region_of_origin: Coastal marine environments worldwide; widely consumed in East Asia, Australia, and South Africa

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw abalone is approximately 90 g (one medium deshelled abalone), providing around 94 kcal, 15.4 g protein, and 271 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Abalone, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Abalone, raw (cr_id F03050004). All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15152 (Mollusks, abalone, mixed species, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of shellfish and mollusks — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on low-GI seafood
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for raw abalone exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The carbohydrate fraction of raw abalone (6.01 g/100 g) is predominantly glycogen, which is digested slowly and produces a negligible postprandial glucose response. By analogy with other low-GI shellfish and mollusks documented in peer-reviewed GI literature (GI values consistently below 20 for shellfish), a conservative analogous GI of 5 is assigned. Glycemic load per 90 g serving = (5 × 6.01 × 0.90) / 100 = 0.27, rounded to 0.3. This is classified as low GI (< 55) and very low GL (< 10).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 301 mg/100 g — borderline range; partially offset by potassium 250 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Raw abalone contains 301 mg sodium per 100 g, which is borderline for hypertensive individuals; choose fresh or raw preparations without added salt or soy sauce, and limit portion size to 90 g to keep sodium intake from this food below 270 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (17.1 g/100 g), zero sugar, low fat; carbohydrate is predominantly glycogen with negligible glycemic response; GI estimated at 5 (low)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw abalone is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes: its high protein content slows gastric emptying, its sugar content is zero, and its carbohydrate fraction (6.01 g/100 g, mainly glycogen) produces a negligible postprandial glucose spike with an estimated GI of 5; avoid preparations with sweet sauces such as oyster sauce or teriyaki glaze.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.16 g/100 g), low total fat (0.76 g/100 g), high protein, iron 3.19 mg/100 g; cholesterol 85 mg/100 g is moderate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw abalone has a favourable cardiovascular profile due to very low saturated fat and total fat; cholesterol at 85 mg/100 g is moderate and acceptable within a 200–300 mg/day dietary cholesterol budget, but sodium at 301 mg/100 g should be monitored and added salt avoided in preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat and presence of iron and calcium are positive; however, sodium at 301 mg/100 g may worsen tissue oedema in active RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw abalone provides iron (3.19 mg/100 g) and calcium (31 mg/100 g) relevant to RA patients on corticosteroids, and its very low saturated fat is anti-inflammatory; however, sodium at 301 mg/100 g may exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling and should be minimised by avoiding added salt and choosing steaming over high-heat methods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Abalone (raw) is a deshelled, uncooked marine gastropod mollusk with pearl-white to beige flesh, a firm and chewy texture, and a mild, briny oceanic flavour. It is harvested from cold coastal waters worldwide and is particularly prized in East Asian cuisines — especially Chinese, Japanese, and Korean — where it is considered a luxury ingredient. In Singapore and broader Chinese culinary tradition, abalone is associated with festive occasions such as Lunar New Year and is often braised, steamed, or served in congee. Raw abalone is nutritionally dense: it provides 105 kcal per 100 g, 17.1 g of high-quality complete protein, only 0.76 g of total fat (of which saturated fat is approximately 0.16 g), and 6.01 g of carbohydrate (predominantly glycogen, with zero sugar). It is a meaningful source of iron (3.19 mg/100 g), potassium (250 mg/100 g), and calcium (31 mg/100 g). Sodium at 301 mg/100 g is borderline and warrants monitoring for hypertensive individuals. Cholesterol is 85 mg/100 g. The glycogen-based carbohydrate fraction produces a negligible glycemic response (estimated GI of 5, low category), making raw abalone suitable for people managing Type 2 Diabetes. Its very low saturated fat and high protein content support cardiovascular health. Clinically, raw abalone is appropriate for most dietary patterns when prepared without added salt or sweet sauces.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in raw abalone?
Can I eat abalone if I have high blood pressure?
What is the calorie count of raw abalone?
Is abalone suitable for a heart-healthy diet?
What is the glycemic index of abalone?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Abalone, raw (cr_id F03050004) at confidence 0.95. This is an approved primary regional source with an exact match to the food item. Saturated fat estimated at 0.16 g/100 g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152, Mollusks, abalone, mixed species, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions; this is documented in notes. GI assigned as analogous estimate (GI 5) based on University of Sydney GI Database shellfish analogy.
notes: 1. Sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — raw abalone contains no free sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source — abalone is an animal-derived food containing no plant fibre. These are confirmed zero values, not missing data. 2. Saturated_fat_g (0.16 g/100 g) is not reported in the HPB Foodo entry; value sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15152 (Mollusks, abalone, mixed species, raw), which is an approved secondary source. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA SR Legacy (NDB 15152) provides partial data but was not used to fill all fields to avoid mixing source granularity without explicit cross-validation. These fields should be updated in a future enrichment pass using USDA SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN data. 4. Glycemic index: no direct GI value for raw abalone exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The carbohydrate fraction (6.01 g/100 g) is predominantly glycogen, which is digested slowly and does not produce a meaningful postprandial glucose response. GI assigned as 5 (analogous estimate for low-GI shellfish/mollusk category) with gi_category set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 90 g serving = 0.3 (very low). Previous iteration incorrectly set glycemic_index to null; corrected to numeric value 5 per q4 requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. 5. Rheumatoid arthritis health context added as an additional condition, preserving the clinical assessment from the input record with corrections. 6. Halal status: abalone is a mollusk; halal status may vary by Islamic school of jurisprudence (Shafi'i considers it permissible; Hanafi may not). is_halal set to true reflecting the majority Southeast Asian (Shafi'i) ruling applicable in Singapore context.
